title: Saarthak Bakshi vs Dr. Seema Bajaj & Anr on 30 August, 2024
body: Saarthak Bakshi vs Dr. Seema Bajaj & Anr on 30 August, 2024 Author: Navin Chawla Bench: Navin Chawla $~12 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(OS) 148/2019 SAARTHAK BAKSHI .....Plaintiff Through: Mr.Bharat Malhotra, Adv. versus DR. SEEMA BAJAJ & ANR. .....Defendants Through: Mr.Pradeep Chandra & Mr.Parth Dhawan, Advs. CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE NAVIN CHAWLA ORDER % 30.08.2024 I.A. 23317/2023 1. This application has been rendered infructuous and is disposed of as such. I.A. 37385/2024 2. Issue notice. 3. Notice is accepted by Mr.Bharat Malhotra, learned counsel on behalf of the plaintiff. 4. Let a reply be filed within a period of four weeks. Rejoinder thereto, if any, be filed within a period of three weeks thereafter. 5. List on 6th December, 2024. O.A. 77/2023 6. This appeal has been filed by the appellants/defendant nos.1 and 2, challenging the order dated 04.08.2023 passed by the learned Joint Registrar (Judicial), closing the right of defendant nos.1 and 2 to file This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 02/09/2024 at 23:54:41 their written statements and affidavits of admission/denial of documents as also a reply to the application under Order VIII Rule 1 read with Rule 10 of the Code of Civil Procedure , 1908 (in short, ' CPC ') filed by the plaintiff. 7. The learned counsel for the appellants submits that the parties were exploring the possibility of arriving at an amicable settlement of their disputes and, in fact, had entered into a Settlement Deed dated 01.11.2019 recording the terms thereof. On 28.02.2020, a statement was also made before the learned Joint Registrar (Judicial) about the settlement, and based on that statement, the suit was directed to be listed before this Court. Thereafter, there were restrictions in the Court's functioning due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and the matter could not be listed. This Court, in its order dated 17.11.2021, recorded that though an application had been filed by the plaintiff seeking withdrawal of the suit, the application had not been signed by the plaintiff. The application was, therefore, dismissed by this Court as being improper. By an order of 16.02.2022, the parties were again referred to mediation to explore the possibility of arriving at a settlement. The settlement, however, could not take place, and the mediation proceedings were closed, as is recorded in the order dated 31.08.2022 passed by the learned Joint Registrar (Judicial). The learned counsel for the defendants/appellants submits that, therefore, the written statement could not be filed by the defendants, firstly on account of the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic and resultant restricted functioning of the Court, for which limitation was extended till 28.05.2022 in terms of the orders passed by the Supreme Court in This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 02/09/2024 at 23:54:41 RE: Cognizance for Extension of Limitation in Suo Moto, Writ Petition (Civil) 3/2020, and thereafter because of a change in the counsel of the defendants. He submits that the written statement was filed by the earlier counsel on 07.12.2022, however, it could not be brought on record and remained in objection. A fresh written statement was, therefore, finally filed only in November 2023. 8. The learned counsel for the defendants/appellants submits that this being a non-commercial suit, the prohibition on the power of the Court for extending the time for filing of the written statement beyond 120 days is not applicable. He places reliance on the judgment of the Supreme Court in Kailash v. Nanhku , (2005) 4 SCC 480, and also of this Court in Amarendra Dhari Singh v. R.C. Nursery Private Limited , 2023 SCC OnLine Del 84. 9. He also refers to Section 129 of the CPC to submit that the power of the High Court to make rules as to the original civil procedure is circumscribed by the Letters Patent and cannot be inconsistent thereof. Referring to Rule 13 of the Letters Patent (Lahore) (Punjab, Haryana, and Delhi), he submits that the High Court is ordained to follow the law or equity which would have been applied to a civil case of any local Court having jurisdiction as of the date of the promulgation of the said Letters Patent. He submits that Clause 27 of the Letters Patent would not denude from the above position. 10. On the other hand, the learned counsel for the plaintiff submits that the time for filing of the written statement can be extended only by a period of 90 days, in accordance with the provisions of the Delhi High Court Rules, specifically Rule 4 of Chapter VII. He submits that This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 02/09/2024 at 23:54:42 the Division Bench of this Court in its judgment in Manhar Sabharwal v. High Court of Delhi & Ors., NC:2024:DHC:6406, has upheld Rule 4 of Chapter VII of the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules, while also considered the effect of Section 129 of the CPC. 11. I have considered the submissions made by the learned counsel for the parties. 12. In Manhar Sabharwal (supra), the Division Bench of this Court considered the effect of Rule 4 of Chapter VII of the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules, 2018, and the use of the phrase 'but not thereafter' therein, and held that the said phrase provides for an action which is mandatory in nature. It was further held that the Delhi High Court (Original Side), being Special Rules, shall prevail over the provisions of the CPC , which are general in nature. It was held that the power of framing of the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules is traceable to Section 7 of the Delhi High Court Act, which confers authority on the High Court to make rules with respect to practice and procedure for the exercise of its original civil jurisdiction. The Division Bench held as under:- "15. The position that emerges is that Rule 4 of Chapter VII of DHC Original Side Rules, has been framed under Section 129 of the CPC and Section 7 of the DHC Act. Section 129 of the CPC empowers the High Court to regulate its own procedure in exercise of its civil jurisdiction. Section 7 of the DHC Act further empowers this Court to make Rules and Orders with respect to practice and procedure for exercise of its ordinary original civil jurisdiction. The DHC Original Side Rules, being special law, will prevail over the CPC , and have an overriding effect over the general This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 02/09/2024 at 23:54:43 provisions of the CPC . xxxxx 17. The High Court is within its authority and jurisdiction to frame Rules of practice and procedure as to its original civil procedure. The very fact that such an authority has been conferred on the High Court, and such a provision exists in the CPC , which confers such authority on the High Court, envisions difference in the Rules of practice and procedure between a High Court, and a Civil Court." 13. The Court also dealt with the judgment of the Supreme Court in Kailash v. Nanhku (supra) and distinguished the same, by observing as under:- "18.1 The reliance by the petitioners on the judgment in the case of Kailash Vs. Nanhku (supra), is totally misplaced. The said judgment was in the context of interpretation of Order VIII Rule 1 of CPC . Rule 4, Chapter VII of DHC Original Side Rules was not a subject matter of discussion in the said judgment . Even otherwise, Rule 4, Chapter VII of DHC Original Side Rules itself was introduced only in the year 2018. Consequently, in view of Section 129 of CPC, the Original Side Rules of the High Court prevail over the provisions of the CPC . 18.2 The position in the present case is totally different as Section 129 of CPC, which empowers the High Court to frame its own Original Side Rules, is a non-obstante clause and itself excludes the operation of other provisions of the CPC , while conferring jurisdiction on the High Court to frame its Rules. Thus, in case of any inconsistency between the provisions of the DHC Original Side Rules and the provisions of the CPC , the DHC Original Side Rules shall prevail. The judgment relied upon by the petitioners is not applicable to the facts and circumstances of the present cases." This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 02/09/2024 at 23:54:43 14. The Court held that Rule 4 of Chapter 7 of the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules cannot be challenged on account of being contrary to the CPC . The Court held as under:- "21. Section 129 CPC expressly gives the power to the High Court to make Rules, notwithstanding the provisions of the CPC , meaning thereby, the High Court is in its authority to frame Rules that may be contrary to other provisions of the CPC . Therefore, relying on judicial interpretations of the provisions of the CPC , to challenge Rule 4 Chapter VII of DHC Original Side Rules, is totally fallacious. 22. The petitioners have not challenged Section 129 CPC, which refers to the Rules framed by a High Court, having overriding effect over the provisions of the CPC , in view of the non-obstante clause contained therein. The petitioners have also not challenged Section 7 of the DHC Act that empowers the High Court to make Rules and Orders, with respect to Practice and Procedure for exercise of its ordinary original civil jurisdiction. Thus, when plenary powers of this Court to frame the Original Side Rules, are recognized and accepted, the petitioners have not been able to establish any case that the exercise of such powers by this Court, and the Rules framed thereunder, are unconstitutional in any manner." 15. Applying the ratio of the above judgment, it is apparent that Rule 4 of Chapter 7 of the Delhi High Court Rules prescribes the outer limit by which the delay in filing of the written statement may be condoned by the High Court in the exercise of its discretionary power under Order VIII Rule 1 of the CPC . The same cannot be exercised for a delay of beyond 90 days or 120 days in all, even for a non- This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 02/09/2024 at 23:54:44 commercial suit. 16. In the present case, even giving the benefit of the extension of limitation for filing the written statement to be as expiring on 28.05.2022 in terms of the orders of the Supreme Court in RE: Cognizance for Extension of Limitation in Suo Moto Writ Petition (Civil) 3/2020 referred hereinabove, as the written statement was originally filed on 07.12.2022, the period of 90 days would have expired by then. Therefore, this Court does not have the power to condone the delay of the defendants in filing its written statement. Even otherwise, I do not find any justified cause supplied by the defendant for the delay in filing of the written statement. The settlement talks between the parties had admittedly failed on 31.08.2022. 17. Accordingly, the appeal is dismissed. CS(OS) 148/2019 & I.A. 19308/2022 18. List on 6th December, 2024. NAVIN CHAWLA, J AUGUST 30, 2024/rv/as Click here to check corrigendum, if any This is a digitally signed order.
